# Loredex dogfooding findings

## 2026-07-09 — AI Engine developer (streaming sprint, task 1)

Session: implemented `run_stream()` in `src/engine.py`, wrote
`docs/streaming-design.md`, routed it, handed off to nimbus-backend
("expose streaming replies over the public API (SSE), update the API
contract"), synced.

### What worked well

- The session-start habit (`loredex handoffs`) is cheap and instant; "no open
  handoffs for nimbus-ai-engine" is a clear all-clear signal, and the command
  pulls the vault remote first so it is trustworthy.
- Routing was one command and correctly derived the vault location from
  frontmatter: `projects/nimbus-ai-engine/streaming/2026-07-09-streaming-design.md`.
  It also auto-committed the vault, so vault history stays tidy without me
  thinking about it.
- The LLM handoff brief was genuinely good: it summarized the design note
  accurately, distinguished the stable inner contract (chunk event shape)
  from the open work (SSE wire format, API contract update), generated
  concrete next actions, and cited the actual implementation commit
  (76a67f0) even though my note never mentioned a hash — it clearly looked
  at repo git state. Completed well under the 120s budget; no --no-llm
  fallback needed.
- The handoff auto-synced to the remote and was immediately visible from the
  backend repo with `loredex handoffs` — receiver-side discovery is solid.

### Friction

1. No outgoing-handoff visibility (high). After handing off, `loredex
   handoffs` from my own repo says "no open handoffs" — the command only
   lists inbound. There is no `handoffs --sent`, no status column, nothing.
   To learn whether backend received/consumed my handoff I must either run
   `handoffs --project nimbus-backend` (impersonating the receiver), open
   the vault file and read its `status: open` frontmatter, or grep vault git
   log for a consume commit. A desktop app must show a sender-side "Sent
   handoffs" panel: status (open/consumed), who consumed it, when, and what
   they did next. This is the single biggest visibility hole.
2. `route` prints no destination (medium). Output was just "routed 1
   note(s) into <vault>". I had to `find` the vault to learn where the note
   landed. Print the per-note destination path.
3. gitattributes bug in the vault (medium, real bug). loredex writes
   `Start\ Here\ -\ Product.md merge=loredex-generated` into the vault's
   `.git/info/attributes`; gitattributes does not support backslash-escaped
   spaces, so every vault git operation prints "Here\ is not a valid
   attribute name: .git/info/attributes:2" and the merge driver silently
   never applies to that file. Patterns with spaces need quoting:
   `"Start Here - Product.md"`.
4. Routing dirties the source repo after commit (low/medium). `route`
   stamps `loredex: routed` and reformats frontmatter (date quoted, tags
   flow to block style) in the source file, so the note I had just committed
   became dirty again and needed a second chore commit.
5. Handoff footer suggests a different invocation (low). The generated
   handoff ends with `npx -y loredex@latest handoffs --consume <name>`,
   which differs from the installed entrypoint used in this environment.
6. Cross-team verification means leaving the terminal-of-record (medium).
   Verifying "did the handoff land", "what does the vault look like", "did
   sync push" all meant cd-ing into other repos/the vault and running git
   there. No single place shows vault state + handoff flows + sync status.

### Desktop-app implications

- Handoff tracker as a first-class screen: inbound/outbound lanes per
  project, status chips (open/consumed), consumer identity + timestamp,
  linked reading list, generated next-actions as checkable items.
- After a route: inline reveal of where the note landed, click to open it in
  vault context (topic folder, backlinks, index).
- Sync/git surface: vault ahead/behind remote, last sync time, and surfaced
  git warnings (like the attributes bug) instead of buried ones.
- GitHub touchpoints: the LLM brief already correlates handoffs with commits
  (76a67f0). Make that structural — link handoffs to the commits/branch/PR
  that produced the work, and later to the receiving team's PR that consumed
  it (e.g. the backend PR that edits openapi.yaml).
- API-contract touchpoints: this handoff's objective is literally "update
  the API contract", but loredex has no model that openapi.yaml /
  postman_collection.json live in nimbus-backend as source of truth. Model
  contract files as owned artifacts so a handoff can link to the file, its
  owner team, and the endpoint diff when the consuming PR lands.
